title: Why 1x1 Convs Matter Here
concept: Inside Inception modules, 1x1 convolutions are used as cheap learned squeeze layers that reduce channel depth before expensive 3x3 and 5x5 branches run.

exercises.prompt: Recap from Unit 100: Why can a 1x1 convolution be useful before a larger convolution?

options.text: It can reduce channel depth cheaply before the expensive spatial work happens
It increases image resolution by adding new pixels
It removes the need for learned weights

exercises.explain: Exactly — Inception reuses that same squeeze trick inside its branches.

cards.title: A raw 5x5 branch gets expensive fast
cards.body: Parallel branches sound great until you remember that a 5x5 convolution on a deep feature map can cost a lot. GoogLeNet's team needed a way to keep those branches informative without letting computation explode.

cards.title: The squeeze before the expensive branch
cards.body: In an Inception module, a 1x1 convolution often runs before a 3x3 or 5x5 branch. Its job is to reduce the number of channels first. Then the larger spatial filter works on that smaller channel stack, which saves a lot of computation.

cards.title: A narrow doorway before a costly room
cards.body: Imagine a giant workshop where every person inside increases the cost of running the machines. Before sending people into the expensive room, you guide them through a smart doorway that reduces the group to the most useful team. The 1x1 squeeze is that doorway for channels.

cards.title: Why the math changes so much
cards.body: Suppose a branch receives 128 channels and wants a 5x5 conv producing 32 output channels. Direct cost per pixel is about 5×5×128×32 = 102,400. If a 1x1 squeeze first reduces 128 -> 16, that costs 1×1×128×16 = 2,048, and the following 5x5 costs 5×5×16×32 = 12,800. Total: 14,848 — far cheaper than the direct branch.

cards.title: The squeeze is learned, not random
cards.body: A 1x1 squeeze does not randomly throw away channels. It is a learned weighted mix across channels, so the network can compress the input into a smaller, still-useful representation before paying for bigger spatial filters.

cards.title: Direct branch vs. squeezed branch
cards.body: Same branch goal, much smaller bill once channels are squeezed first.

left.text:
128 channels go straight in
Large spatial filter sees full depth
High compute cost

right.text:
128 → 16 channels first
Then 5x5 runs cheaper
Learned compression saves cost

exercises.prompt: Why does GoogLeNet place 1x1 convolutions before some 3x3 and 5x5 branches?

options.text: To reduce channel depth before the expensive larger kernels run
To make the image file smaller on disk
To increase height and width before pooling
To guarantee every branch sees only one channel forever

exercises.explain: Correct — the 1x1 layer is a cheap learned squeeze that makes the next branch affordable.

exercises.prompt: True or false: a 1x1 squeeze usually changes channel depth much more than it changes spatial height and width.
exercises.explain: True — in this role it mainly compresses channels while leaving the spatial grid aligned for the next branch layer.

exercises.sentence: Before an expensive branch runs, a 1x1 conv can reduce channel ___.
exercises.explain: Right — squeezing channel depth is the key savings trick.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best describes the role of 1x1 convolutions inside Inception?

options.text: They often act as learned bottlenecks that squeeze channels before larger spatial branches run
They replace every 3x3 and 5x5 branch so the module no longer processes space
They are included only for visual symmetry and do not affect computation

exercises.explain: Exactly — cheap channel control is what makes the wider module practical.

exercises.prompt: An Inception branch receives a feature map with 128 channels. A 1x1 conv with 24 filters is applied before a 3x3 conv. How many channels enter the 3x3 conv?
exercises.explain: Correct — the 1x1 layer's number of filters sets the new channel depth, so the expensive 3x3 branch only processes 24 channels instead of all 128. Next: how this design gives richer features without paying the naive full cost.
